words: SAM SCHRAGER: Your experience going to the University.
MARIE LEE LEW: Oh, just the usual. I didn't have any trouble. You see, I started
high school here, when my father and mother bought the restaurantfin'26
the year I started high school, 1926. Ijust graduated from grade
school in Spokane and then we move over here in '26. I started grade
school in 1920. And finished in '26 and I started here in '26 and finish
ed in '29 at Moscow High School.

speaker: SS
words: What were you interested in studying there?

speaker: MLL
words: In home economics. That's what I graduated- the department, of Home Econ

speaker: MLL
words: omics. I've always wanted to teach home economics when Iwas agirl, in

speaker: MLL
words: my young days. And so, I thought I would go into that and teach that

speaker: MLL
words: when Ifinished school. So beginning in high school I was interested in

speaker: MLL
words: going into that.

speaker: SS
words: There was alady who was in that department for along time.

speaker: MLL
words: Miss Jensen.

speaker: SS
words: Miss Jensen. What was she like?

speaker: MLL
words: Uh-huh, Catherine Jensen. Wonderful woman. She died must be about ten,

speaker: MLL
words: fifteen years ago now. But she was the head of the Home Economics Depar

speaker: MLL
words: tment at that time and then there was a Miss Lewis in foods and Miss

speaker: MLL
words: Featherstone.

speaker: SS
words: She's still in Moscow.

speaker: SS
words: She's still in Moscow, but she's not teaching any more. And those are

speaker: SS
words: the ones that Iremember. And Miss Jensen, at that time, she was teach

speaker: SS
words: ing child development and counseling and family, and dietetics and so on

speaker: SS
words: and so forth. And each one of the teachers had.

speaker: SS
words: Did you find that you learned agreat deal in that program?

speaker: MLL
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: SS
words: That was useful?

speaker: MLL
words: Oh, yes. I learned a great deal -fh even though I didn't teach after

speaker: MLL
words: I graduated, but it still helped me in our business and in my home; in

speaker: MLL
words: my raising of the family. It has all helped. At the time I figure if I

speaker: MLL
words: never go into teaching, which I was hoping to go into anyway, but if I

speaker: MLL
words: happerf'to get married I could still use that, you see, for myself.

speaker: SS
words: What happened that you didn't go into teaching?

speaker: MLL
words: Oh, I was married two years before I graduated. I was married in 1931

speaker: MLL
words: when my future husband came back. He graduated in 1929 from Washington

speaker: MLL
words: State. He went back to China to look over situations there and the jobs

speaker: MLL
words: and so on and so forth. A far as he could see, there wasn't too

speaker: MLL
words: much future in there at that time, so he came back here in '31.

speaker: SS
words: Did you know each other when he back then?

speaker: MLL
words: Oh, yes. Yes, we knew each other when he was going to school in Pullman.

speaker: MLL
words: He was going to school in Pullman in- from 1925 to 1929.

speaker: SS
words: Did you go together at that time?

speaker: MLL
words: Oh, we knew each other. We saw each other- I mean we see each other at

speaker: MLL
words: school functions between Washington State and Idaho, you know. Football

speaker: MLL
words: games and things. But we didn't actually go together until after he graduated

speaker: MLL
words: from Washington State. And that was in June of 29. And then he

speaker: MLL
words: stayed here for the summer. I think he was taking a little extra curri

speaker: MLL
words: culum during summer school all that summer although he graduated, but he

speaker: MLL
words: thought he'd like to spend the time here going to summer school and pro

speaker: MLL
words: bably knowing me a little bit better, maybe at the time. But he had de

speaker: MLL
words: cided that he would go back to China in the fall of '29 to look for a

speaker: MLL
words: job and see what the situation was over there at that time. When he went

speaker: MLL
words: back in the fall of '29 until the spring of '31 he\seen that there wasn't

speaker: MLL
words: too much future in his engineering career there at the time, because he

speaker: MLL
words: could almost foresee the Japanese-Chinese War coming on. You know, things

speaker: MLL
words: were very disturbed there and the situation was bad as far as government

speaker: MLL
words: jobs for Civil Service jobs. His field would be building roads and buil

speaker: MLL
words: dings and he doesn't see very much future in that with things coming on.

speaker: SS
words: Would he have had trouble getting a job as an engineer here because he is

speaker: SS
words: Chinese?

speaker: MLL
words: Yes. He would have a harder job to get a job here because he is a Chi

speaker: MLL
words: nese than the American people would- American boys graduating from the

speaker: MLL
words: same class. Because he can see that during his time, like he had said

speaker: MLL
words: before, the small races are discriminated against. And when he went on

speaker: MLL
words: field trips with his school mates he was not allowed to sit in the same

speaker: MLL
words: place in the movies, you know as they were and he was not allowed tothey

speaker: MLL
words: wouldn't give him a haircut at certain places where his schoolmates

speaker: MLL
words: were allowed to have haircuts, you know. And I don't think he ever tried

speaker: MLL
words: to eat at the same places, but he didn't say very much about discrimination

speaker: MLL
words: there. But he did felt very badly about that all the other school

speaker: MLL
words: mates on these field trips they were able to buy a ticket and go to a

speaker: MLL
words: show, but he could not sit with them. He bought the same ticket and

speaker: MLL
words: paid the same price, but he could not sit with them. He has to be segre

speaker: MLL
words: gated in a different section of the theatre. This was at college, some

speaker: MLL
words: where around '28 and '29. That is not in Pullman, .. I'm not say

speaker: MLL
words: ing that, but on field trips like when he went to Spokane. Especially

speaker: MLL
words: when he went to Spokane. At that place he definitely remember the Pantages

speaker: MLL
words: Theatre in Spokane at that time was opening, and he was not allowed

speaker: MLL
words: to go in with them.

speaker: SS
words: Was it a section where anyone that was a minority group sat, like black

speaker: SS
words: people, too?

speaker: MLL
words: Yes. Black people or Indian or Negroes. And Chinese have to be there, too.

speaker: MLL
words: And only whites could sit where the rest of the people sit.

speaker: SS
words: What about for you? If you had wanted to become a teacher, would it

speaker: SS
words: have been tough for you at that time?

speaker: ML
words: I think it would have been a little bit tough for me, too. It certain

speaker: ML
words: ly wouldn't be as easy as it is for my daughters to be getting a teach

speaker: ML
words: ing job now. Nowdays, they look at your record and they give you a job

speaker: ML
words: according to your record in school, but in those days, well, if you're

speaker: ML
words: not a— at that time we were not American citizens because we were not

speaker: ML
words: allowed to become American citizens until 1943. So, when I graduated in

speaker: ML
words: 1933 I was not an American citizen yet. But, of course, I never tried

speaker: ML
words: to get a job as a teacher, because, like I say, I went into the restau

speaker: ML
words: rant business in '32. Well, when my husband and I were married we went

speaker: ML
words: into the restaurant business together, and he need me to help him there,

speaker: ML
words: and so, I never looked for a job. But of course, I didn't graduate un

speaker: ML
words: til two years afterwards.

speaker: SS
words: But you continued to go to school?

speaker: MLL
words: Yes, I continued and finished school in 1933.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of arrangement did you make with your father to take over the

speaker: SS
words: business at that time? Did he sell it to you?

speaker: MLL
words: Yes. We bought it from him just like anybody else would buy it. Just

speaker: MLL
words: like he would sell to anybody else, with the stipulation, of course, that

speaker: MLL
words: we couldn't pay him all at once, so we had to pay him in monthly instal

speaker: MLL
words: lments. Because when we started we didn't have any money. I was still

speaker: MLL
words: going to school at that time and although the tuition, you know, wasn't

speaker: MLL
words: very high, but it still cost a little bit of money and during that time

speaker: MLL
words: money was scarce. We were married in the summer of 1931 and the fall of

speaker: MLL
words: '31 was not too bad, but beginning March of '32 when all the banks were

speaker: MLL
words: closed, that was really bad then. In fact, y third year was beginning

speaker: MLL
words: to be bad already. was not as good as it had been before and things

speaker: MLL
words: were slowing down, and there wasn't too much business at the timee

speaker: MLL
words: bought the restaurant over. But like everything else there wasn't very

speaker: MLL
words: much business anyplace else either. So, at that time, for what money

speaker: MLL
words: we d,d not have, that was the best situation we could get, was to buy

speaker: MLL
words: from my folks because they wanted to sell the place because they wanted

speaker: MLL
words: to take the other children, the younger children back to China to give

speaker: MLL
words: them alittle bit of Chinese culture when they were still young.

speaker: SS
words: Did they do that?

speaker: MLL
words: Yes, they did that.

speaker: SS
words: So they went back and did they stay or come back here?

speaker: SS
words: Yes they stay. But in 1937 when the Japanese-Chinese War in Shanghai-

speaker: SS
words: Nanking Nanking in my folks settled in after they went back there, because they

speaker: SS
words: see that the future was better in Nankinthan down south in Canton; that

speaker: SS
words: is, for them. So they settled up in the north and then they stay there

speaker: SS
words: until the Japanese-Chinese Var started and then they sent my two younger

speaker: SS
words: brother and sister back here to the States. They being born here in the

speaker: SS
words: United States, being citizenS/ they were able to get passage back as

speaker: SS
words: soon as they could get room on the ships. But at that time the ships

speaker: SS
words: was all crowded, so they had to wait about a month or six weeks before

speaker: SS
words: they could- about amonth, anyway. And they had to go from Shanghaithey

speaker: SS
words: were hoping to get a ship from Shanghai to come over to the United

speaker: SS
words: States, but not being able to, they went south and went down to Canton

speaker: SS
words: and to Hong Kong, and they were finally able to getassage there. Be

speaker: SS
words: cause everybody who (waS) U. S. citizens, you know, they were coming back

speaker: SS
words: over here to the States then, and the ships werall crowded. But they

speaker: SS
words: did finally get back here and Mi and I, beifng thenly.ones over here,

speaker: SS
words: you know, that we could look after them, why naturally they sent my brother and sister

speaker: SS
words: over here in 1937 for us to take care of them.

speaker: SS
words: Was it hard for you to keep going to school during the Depression?

speaker: SS
words: Well, it wasn't too hard, no, since I have my board at the restaurant

speaker: SS
words: and my husband I have an apartment in town, we were able to have enough

speaker: SS
words: tuition, you know,for me to go school. Of course, I kept working at

speaker: SS
words: the restaurant and helping Mi, and we didn't have to hire too many help

speaker: SS
words: 0 at that time. Well, during the Depression 7just don't have too much

speaker: SS
words: business, but we still had one extra cook that we hired and one or two

speaker: SS
words: waitresses. We want to keep them although we weren't able to pay them

speaker: SS
words: every month, you know, all their wages, they were willing to stay on,

speaker: SS
words: because there wasn't any other jobs any place else for them either. So

speaker: SS
words: as long as they stay on with us, if and when condition gets better and

speaker: SS
words: we can afford to pay therefor their backwages, why we would, they know

speaker: SS
words: that we would, and so they stayed with us.

speaker: SS
words: Did that happen finally? That you could pay them?

speaker: SS
words: Oh, yes. Yes, we pay up allhat we owed, you know. Well, when you've

speaker: SS
words: never had too much money to spend, you know, we just don't have the habit

speaker: SS
words: of spending any money. As agirl, well, we were not exactly very poor,

speaker: SS
words: but we were not rich, either, you know. Just aaverage family. My

speaker: SS
words: father have a business- as agirl, my father just have abusiness and

speaker: SS
words: Iwas always- us children always have to help in the restaurant business.

speaker: SS
words: So what would you spend on? What would it be? Just clothing?

speaker: SS
words: Just very little on clothing. What have to be spent, like books and

speaker: SS
words: things we had to have, well, we have to buy those. And whatever there's

speaker: SS
words: alittle bit left over, then, it goes into clothing. And at that time

speaker: SS
words: Iknow we didn't even get to go to a show very often. Not even once a

speaker: SS
words: week at that time. Well in the first place, you know, when there isn't

speaker: SS
words: too much business and not too much money, you have to work a little bit

speaker: SS
words: harder, you get pretty tired, you know. By the end of the working day

speaker: SS
words: in the restaurant you want to go home and get some rest and sleep.

speaker: SS
words: And you were going to school.

speaker: MLL
words: And I was going to school, too, in the meantime.

speaker: SS
words: Was there much social life when you were going to school?

speaker: MLL
words: At that time the Chinese girls and boys doesn't have too much social life

speaker: MLL
words: with the American people. But we do have what we call International Club

speaker: MLL
words: you know. What was it Mi?

speaker: ML
words: Cosmopolitan.

speaker: MLL
words: Yeah, Cosmopolitan Club. At that time there were quite afew

speaker: MLL
words: ilip ino boys and some Chinese boys, but mostly boys sent over here

speaker: MLL
words: from their own country. Now the Chinese people have some scholarships

speaker: MLL
words: from the, Ithink it's from the Boxer Rebellion, quite afew years back,

speaker: MLL
words: that they would still allow some Chinese boys to come over here to st udy.

speaker: MLL
words: Although these boys wouldn't be from apoor family, they from about

speaker: MLL
words: average families that have a certain amount of money to buy their clothes.

speaker: MLL
words: You know the scholarship is only for board and room- maybe for part of

speaker: MLL
words: the room and all tuition fees, like out of state and'all that. But

speaker: MLL
words: from their own family, they have to support them otherwise. So as far

speaker: MLL
words: as we're concerned, that's about the only social we have and we do

speaker: MLL
words: have ministers from Moscow and Pullman that work with us, you know, in

speaker: MLL
words: our group that are interested in us. And one was Reverend Drury from

speaker: MLL
words: the Presbyterian Church. He's the one that married us in 1931, because

speaker: MLL
words: we know them pretty well and he was with our group in the Cosmopolitan,

speaker: MLL
words: and he was interested in the foreign students, you know, and there was

speaker: MLL
words: quite afew of the professors, too, that are interested, and they sponsor

speaker: MLL
words: us.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of activities would you have? Social ones. Would they be

speaker: SS
words: dances and things like that?

speaker: MLL
words: Not too much dancing because we don't do too much dancing. But we get

speaker: MLL
words: into a group and we would have coffee or tea and a little ice cream or

speaker: MLL
words: cake or something. And then we would talk, you know, and we would dis

speaker: MLL
words: cussions, mostly our own school work ,and our concerns with our everyday

speaker: MLL
words: activity, and with the church, maybe. Very little in politics, because

speaker: MLL
words: we have no vote, anyway. But we do, and we are interested in what's

speaker: MLL
words: going on. At least, we know what's happening anyway, especially around

speaker: MLL
words: our two— quite often we have the joint Cosmopolitan Clubs between Washington

speaker: MLL
words: State and Idaho, and we would go out on picnics and we would go out

speaker: MLL
words: on hikes, you know, up to Moscow Mountain, or some place like that and we

speaker: MLL
words: would make a whole day of it either on a Sunday or on a Saturday when the

speaker: MLL
words: weather's good.

speaker: SS
words: But few girls; mostly boys.

speaker: MLL
words: Yes. There are just a few girls.

speaker: SS
words: Is that because there were few that had come with scholarships?

speaker: MLL
words: Yes, most of the families would send their boys out to get educated, be

speaker: MLL
words: cause usually they figured that the boys are the ones to make the living

speaker: MLL
words: for the family, and they should get educated first.

speaker: SS
words: And it was mostly ino people and Chinese? And were there Japanese

speaker: SS
words: too?

speaker: MLL
words: Not too many Japanese. At that time the ilip inos, as far as I know,

speaker: MLL
words: when they come here they have a different status, because, you see, they

speaker: MLL
words: were under U.S. control, and I think they could come and go without too

speaker: MLL
words: much red tape. The Chinese, and I don't know about the Japanese, I think

speaker: MLL
words: they come a little bit easier even, but the Chinese have a very hard time

speaker: MLL
words: getting over; red tape and all that. And they can only come over as a

speaker: MLL
words: student and they can only go as a student and they cannot work over here.

speaker: MLL
words: And as soon as they are through school, they get their diploma and then

speaker: MLL
words: they have to go back.

speaker: SS
words: That's Japanese?

speaker: MLL
words: Chinese.

speaker: SS
words: Chinese, too?

speaker: MLL
words: Uh-huh. Idon't know too much about the Japanese. The Japanese people

speaker: MLL
words: Iknow they can come over here alittle bit easier than Chinese. They

speaker: MLL
words: have a better quota. And I heard, if the Japanese Government would allow

speaker: MLL
words: them to come over, they could almost get 100 sure that they would allow

speaker: MLL
words: them to come over here to go to school.

speaker: SS
words: This is now, or back then?

speaker: MLL
words: Then. Even then, so now it should be more liberal.

speaker: SS
words: So that means really, that the people were not- were expected

speaker: SS
words: not to go to the dances and those things that were on campus?

speaker: MLL
words: Yes. Idon't know, Idon't think there are too many of the dances where

speaker: MLL
words: they would mix. As far as Iknow, Iwas the only Chinese girl going to

speaker: MLL
words: the University at the time. And as far as Iknow, there were only two

speaker: MLL
words: at the most three, Chinese girls at Washington State at that time. So

speaker: MLL
words: there aren't too many giis, as far as Chinese girls are concerned. And

speaker: MLL
words: Japanese were about the same. And I'm not saying anything very much about

speaker: MLL
words: the Vietnamese or any, because at that time Idon't think we had too

speaker: MLL
words: many of those people. comiJ over,

speaker: SS
words: Pretty strict.

speaker: MLL
words: Pretty strict segregation, yes. I think so, as far as school was concerned.

speaker: MLL
words: Well, how did that make you feel at the time? Because it seems to me,

speaker: MLL
words: besides being unfair, it seems kind of difficult to expect people to-

speaker: MLL
words: Well, it didn't to me because- for one thing, it makes our race of students study

speaker: MLL
words: a little bit harder because they have more time to study.

speaker: MLL
words: They have less time to go out socially then. That's one good thing out

speaker: MLL
words: of it. For another thing, now, well, I myself, I am more or less an in

speaker: MLL
words: trovert and I don't go out and well, barge myself into other things. I

speaker: MLL
words: don't care too much for dancing, because I never had it. Well, I never

speaker: MLL
words: went out, you know. I was pretty well kept at home during my grade school

speaker: MLL
words: and high school years and in college I was still staying home, under the

speaker: MLL
words: supervision of my mother and father. And, being Chinese, you know, I

speaker: MLL
words: just go to school to go to school; to learn. And besides I have to spend

speaker: MLL
words: some time in work, too, you know, to help the folks out.

speaker: SS
words: Well do you think the kids in the Cosmopolitan Club that they- I can

speaker: SS
words: imagine if it was me, I imagine tttfV I would react by feeling that I was

speaker: SS
words: better than all those other kids anyway. And I'm just wondering if that

speaker: SS
words: was the way that some of the kids, maybe would feel.

speaker: MLL
words: You think that theyare a little bit better than the American people?

speaker: SS
words: No. I was thinking that that's the natural way that it would be to react.

speaker: MLL
words: No.

speaker: SS
words: To think that, because that's how I imagine I would feel. That's the

speaker: SS
words: way I have felt when I have been excluded from a group and that sort of

speaker: SS
words: thing.

speaker: MLL
words: They think they're so much better. Well, as far as I am concerned, they

speaker: MLL
words: can think they're that much better, it's alright with me, you know, be

speaker: MLL
words: cause it doesn't matter to me. What I want, at that time, is mostly an

speaker: MLL
words: education, anyway. The faster I get it, the better I am off. Maybe the

speaker: MLL
words: sooner I can get out there and get into a better job maybe, bring in more

speaker: MLL
words: money for myself and for my family. So I don't miss it.

speaker: SS
words: How much friendship was extended to you from the— like the other girls

speaker: SS
words: that were in home Were they very outgoing and friendly to you, or

speaker: SS
words: did they sort of leave you alone?

speaker: MLL
words: Oh, no. Home economics is all girls, anyway at that time. So, as far

speaker: MLL
words: as I'm a girl, I am just one of the girls with them; with my own home

speaker: MLL
words: ec girls. I don't think I was excluded from any of their things. I go

speaker: MLL
words: to all of their— whatever is going on, I go to it. And I was invited

speaker: MLL
words: to all of them. So as far as home economics, the department, is concer

speaker: MLL
words: ned, all the teachers treat me just as well as anybody else. There is

speaker: MLL
words: no distinction; I don't feel any distinction between their attitude

speaker: MLL
words: other students and their attitude toward me. So, as far as I

speaker: MLL
words: am concerned- maybe it's because I took that course particular

speaker: MLL
words: field, because I like it and that's why I took it, and it just happened

speaker: MLL
words: to be one of the things that's mostly all girls. So, I was very satisfied

speaker: MLL
words: with my life in school. And I do have a few very close friends that I

speaker: MLL
words: have made and I have continued with, you know from the Home Economics

speaker: MLL
words: Department and also a few that were not in the Home Economics,

speaker: MLL
words: But the few girls that live in town here in Moscow, because they went to

speaker: MLL
words: high school with me. And I think everybody in Moscow has treated my fa

speaker: MLL
words: mily and me and now my husband and I— afterwards my husband and I- well,

speaker: MLL
words: that is, the people that know us, we were never discriminated against,

speaker: MLL
words: as far as I know. And they were friendly to us, at least to our faces,

speaker: MLL
words: anyway. And, like I say, we're the kind of people that doesn't go out

speaker: MLL
words: too much. We don't associate too much. We don't go for dancing or drin

speaker: MLL
words: king or smoking, so that excludes a lot of stuff from us, you see. We

speaker: MLL
words: just have a few friends that we like to entertain in our own home and

speaker: MLL
words: we go to our friends' homes and we just enjoy ourself that way. And

speaker: MLL
words: businesswise, I think we have been treated very well in Moscow. Well,

speaker: MLL
words: there's always one or two exceptions, you know. Maybe they don't like

speaker: MLL
words: our race in the first place and so they! got that against us, too. And

speaker: MLL
words: so maybe they don't want to deal with us. Well, those people, we just

speaker: MLL
words: don't deal with them. You know, if they don't want to deal with us, we're

speaker: MLL
words: not going to bust in and deal with them because there are other people

speaker: MLL
words: who would deal with us, that we could associate with in business, as far

speaker: MLL
words: as that's concerned. Whenever we need anything— whenever we need any

speaker: MLL
words: thing, we need to buy anything, we need to buy any services, we are all

speaker: MLL
words: well treated. In doctoring, you know the doctors, why we're well treated.

speaker: MLL
words: And department stores, we're well treated and all the supermarkets, gro

speaker: MLL
words: cery stores and the car dealers or the travel angencies, all. You know.

speaker: SS
words: Thinking about your kin and other people you know that were Chinese;

speaker: SS
words: there must be some cases that you know of where really the people didn't

speaker: SS
words: get treated well, when they should have been. At least I imagine there

speaker: SS
words: must have been because of—

speaker: MLL
words: Because of the race?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah.

speaker: MLL
words: Well particularly in my family, I don't know of anybody that wasn't trea

speaker: MLL
words: ted.— You know the Chinese people as a whole, we don't want to butt in
